Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time during the week, but most days I just couldn't bring myself to focus. I'd sit with my materials for hours, maybe 33 hours a week on paper, but a lot of that time I was just staring at the screen feeling overwhelmed. The sadness made it hard to actually absorb anything, so I'd just go through the motions without really engaging.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly depending on when deadlines were looming. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, then basically nothing for days. The anxiety would build up and paralyze me, and then panic would eventually force me to do something. It wasn't healthy, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle no matter how much I told myself to be more consistent.
Q3: Usually it was fear that prompted me to log in. I'd remember an assignment was coming up, or I'd get an email reminder, and the dread would push me to check what I was missing. Sometimes I logged in just to see if I'd missed something important, then immediately felt worse about how behind I was. It was rarely genuine curiosity or interest, just anxiety-driven checking.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a comfortable habit for me. Every time I opened it, I felt this weight on my chest seeing all the things I should have done. I tried to make it routine at the start of the semester, but the negative feelings associated with it made me avoid it more and more. It became something I dreaded rather than something automatic.
Q5: The lecture recordings were probably the most useful because I could listen to them while doing other things or lying in bed when I couldn't face sitting at my desk. Being an auditory learner, hearing the professor explain things helped more than reading. I'd sometimes play them multiple times because my concentration was so poor the first time through. At least with audio I could absorb something even on bad days.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums. The thought of posting something and having people judge it made my anxiety spike. I also ignored a lot of the supplementary readings because I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. The optional practice exercises felt pointless when I couldn't even manage the required work. I just didn't have the mental energy to engage with anything extra.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course. If it counted toward my grade, I'd force myself through it. But if something was just 'for enrichment' or 'recommended,' I couldn't justify the emotional effort. With my motivation so low, everything had to have an immediate, tangible payoff or I just couldn't make myself care enough to do it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, so I had to do them even when every part of me wanted to give up. Optional stuff just added to the pile of things I felt guilty about not doing. The required work already exhausted me completely, so optional activities were basically invisible to me. They just made me feel worse about what I wasn't accomplishing.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the lecture audio files and listened to them on my phone. I'd lie in bed with headphones, sometimes for hours, just letting the lectures play. I also borrowed a classmate's notes once because I couldn't face watching the videos. None of that would show up anywhere in the system. Some of my actual learning happened completely offline, away from the computer that stressed me out.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd listen to downloaded lectures on repeat while trying to fall asleep. I'd also sometimes write notes by hand in a notebook because typing felt like too much effort. When I was really struggling, I'd just read through printed materials in bed rather than logging into anything. The LMS would show I was inactive, but I was still trying to learn in my own limited way.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a lecture recording, then never go back to that page online. I'd access something once to grab it, then study from the downloaded version for days. My login frequency probably looks low, but I was often working from materials I'd already pulled down. It was easier to face a file on my desktop than to log into the whole system.
Q12: Around midterm, everything got harder. The workload piled up and my anxiety became almost unbearable. I started missing classes because I couldn't face leaving my room. The sadness got heavier and I'd spend whole days doing nothing, then feel worse about wasting time. It became this terrible cycle where falling behind made me more anxious, which made it harder to catch up, which made me more anxious.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log in and just stare at the dashboard without clicking anything. Competing responsibilities weren't even the main issue—it was more that my mental state made everything feel impossible. Confusion about what to prioritize would paralyze me. I'd open the LMS intending to work, then close it ten minutes later having done nothing but feel worse about myself.
Q14: Many times. I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed a single thing. My mind would wander to dark places while the lecture played in the background. I'd scroll through readings without any of the words registering. Being logged in and having the material in front of me didn't mean I was actually present or learning. My body was there but my mind was somewhere else entirely.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially discussion posts and group work. The social aspect terrified me. I knew they were important for my grade, but the anxiety of putting myself out there was paralyzing. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come and I'd feel even worse. The avoidance made everything harder in the end, but I couldn't stop doing it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I would have done nothing. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was a day or two away, then scramble in a panic. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates, then nothing. It wasn't strategic—it was just me being too paralyzed to act until the fear of failing outweighed everything else.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag. Sometimes getting a decent grade would briefly lift my mood and I'd think maybe I could do this. But negative feedback or even constructive criticism would send me spiraling. I'd avoid checking my grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When feedback was delayed, I'd just assume the worst and feel anxious about it constantly.
Q18: The weekly structure helped give me some framework, but it also meant constant deadlines that never let up. I couldn't catch my breath between assignments. The course moved forward whether I was ready or not, and I often wasn't. Having clear expectations was good in theory, but when you're struggling to function, even clear expectations feel like impossible demands.
Q19: The heavy reliance on discussion participation made things harder for me. I dreaded posting anything publicly. The self-paced elements were theoretically good, but without external structure I'd just drift. What would have helped is more audio content and maybe some flexibility on deadlines for people who are struggling. The rigidity of the system didn't account for bad mental health days.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but not actually being able to produce anything. You can spend hours on the LMS feeling paralyzed, opening and closing documents, watching videos without absorbing them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just anxious checking and rechecking without the ability to actually complete anything.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to downloaded lectures, reading printed notes, even just thinking about the material while lying in bed. My LMS activity was low, but I still managed to pass because the actual studying happened elsewhere. The system only sees what you do on their platform, not what you do with the knowledge afterward.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they can't show if you were actually mentally present. They miss all the offline studying, the re-reading of downloaded materials, the thinking you do away from the computer. They also can't capture how you felt while engaging—whether you were focused or dissociating, motivated or just going through the motions out of fear.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It means showing up mentally, not just physically. For me, true engagement was rare this semester. Mostly I was just surviving, doing the minimum to not fail. Real engagement would mean curiosity and energy, and I had neither. I was present but not truly engaged most of the time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, how many times I cried over assignments, how the sadness made everything feel pointless. It wouldn't show the hours I spent trying to work up the courage to just open a document. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions I attended trying to cope, or how hard I was fighting just to stay enrolled. The data shows behavior, not the battle behind it.
